findings.title: SQL Injection in login endpoint via template literal interpolation
findings.cwe_name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
findings.code_snippet: const query = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '${username}' AND password = '${password}'`;
findings.description: User-controlled input from req.query.username and req.query.password (lines 13-14) is directly interpolated into a SQL query string via JavaScript template literals on line 16. No parameterized queries, prepared statements, or input sanitization is applied. The query is then executed via db.get() on line 20.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker sends GET /login?username=admin'--&password=anything to bypass password verification entirely. The resulting query becomes SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin'--' AND password = 'anything', which comments out the password check and authenticates as admin. Alternatively, ' OR '1'='1 in either field returns all rows.

findings.title: Hardcoded admin credentials in source code
findings.description: The admin username 'admin' and password 'password123' are hardcoded directly in the source code. Anyone with access to the repository can read these credentials and authenticate as the admin user.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker who gains access to the source code repository (e.g., via a public GitHub repo, leaked source, or insider access) reads line 9 and obtains the admin credentials. They then visit /login?username=admin&password=password123 to authenticate.

findings.title: Plaintext password storage and comparison
findings.cwe_name: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort
findings.description: Passwords are stored in plaintext in the SQLite database (line 9) and compared in plaintext via the SQL query (line 16-20). No hashing algorithm (bcrypt, scrypt, argon2) is used. If the database is ever exposed, all user passwords are immediately compromised.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker who gains any read access to the SQLite database (via SQL injection with UNION SELECT, a backup leak, or server compromise) obtains all passwords in cleartext, enabling account takeover and credential stuffing against other services where users may reuse passwords.

findings.title: Credentials transmitted via GET query string parameters
findings.cwe_name: Use of GET Request Method With Sensitive Query Strings
findings.description: The login endpoint uses GET with credentials passed as query string parameters (req.query.username, req.query.password). GET parameters are recorded in browser history, server access logs, proxy logs, and Referer headers, exposing credentials in multiple locations.
findings.attack_scenario: A user logs in via /login?username=admin&password=password123. The full URL including credentials is stored in the browser history, any intermediate proxy logs, and the web server's access log. An attacker with access to any of these logs (e.g., shared computer, log aggregation system) harvests the credentials.

findings.title: SQL query with user input logged to console
findings.description: The fully interpolated SQL query, which includes unsanitized user input (username and password), is logged to the console on every request. This exposes credentials in server logs and allows log injection via crafted input containing newline characters or log format strings.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker sends /login?username=admin%0ARunning+query:+SELECT+*+FROM+users+WHERE+id=1&password=x to inject fake log entries. Additionally, every legitimate login attempt logs the user's plaintext password to the server console/log files.

findings.title: No rate limiting on login endpoint
findings.cwe_name: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
findings.description: The /login endpoint has no rate limiting, account lockout, or throttling mechanism. An attacker can make unlimited authentication attempts without any delay or blocking.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker runs a brute-force script sending thousands of GET /login requests per second with different password guesses. With no rate limiting or lockout, they can attempt the entire rockyou.txt wordlist in minutes. Given the weak password 'password123', the attack would succeed quickly.

findings.title: No security headers configured
findings.description: The Express application does not set any security headers. No Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, or other security headers are configured. No helmet middleware or equivalent is used.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker embeds the application in an iframe on a malicious site for clickjacking attacks. Without X-Content-Type-Options, browsers may MIME-sniff responses. Without CSP, any future XSS vulnerability becomes more easily exploitable.

summary.scope_notes: Full analysis of all source files in the repository. The application consists of a single Express server file (app.js) with one endpoint and no middleware. All seven findings are confirmed by direct code inspection.
